item.title: Amazon's Last-Mile Push & China's Robotics Rise
item.description: Amazon acquires a stair-climbing delivery bot, while China's industrial ambition builds a broader competitive edge.
item.content:encoded:
Robot Weekly

Amazon's Last-Mile Push & China's Robotics Rise

Mar 22, 2026

Amazon acquires a stair-climbing delivery bot, while China's industrial ambition builds a broader competitive edge.

From Chen Chen, Editor at robot.tv

Editorial Note

This week, a major acquisition shows where one giant is placing its automation bets. Meanwhile, the scale of China's robotics push is becoming clearer. Let's get into it.

Lead Signal

Amazon acquires Rivr, maker of a stair-climbing delivery robot

Amazon has acquired Rivr, a startup developing a stair-climbing delivery robot. The move signals a deeper investment in last-mile automation for complex urban environments.

This is a direct attempt to automate the final, most complex segment of the logistics chain, moving beyond simple curb-side drops.

What Else Mattered

  • Inside China’s robotics revolution

    China's robotics rise looks bigger than a single humanoid headline. Manufacturing depth, policy support, and industrial ambition may be combining into a broader competitive advantage.

    It signals a structural shift in global robotics competition, driven by more than just hype.

  • How humanoid robots joined this factory's workforce

    A factory-floor humanoid story matters most when it shows robots being placed into mixed human-robot workflows with a clearer operating role.

    The real test is integration into existing workflows, not just being a spectacle.

  • Building tomorrow: How Bedrock Robotics is changing the construction industry

    Bedrock Robotics ties robotics ambition to a concrete industry workflow, where customer adoption depends on operating fit rather than broad platform hype.

    It's a useful case study in solving for specific, gritty industry problems first.

What To Watch

That's the briefing for this week. The focus is shifting from what's possible to what works in practice. I'll see you next time.

You’re receiving this because you signed up for Robot Weekly at robot.tv.

Read more at news.robot.tv.

Issue archive: https://robot.tv/newsletters/robot-weekly-2026-03-23.html

robot.tv, 8 The Green, Suite 4000, Dover, DE 19901, USA

item.title: Robot Weekly | Mar 16, 2026
item.description: A concise weekly brief on robotics deployments, company moves, and what matters next.
item.content:encoded:
Robot Weekly

Robot Weekly | Mar 16, 2026

Mar 16, 2026

A concise weekly brief on robotics deployments, company moves, and what matters next.

From Chen Chen, Editor at robot.tv

Editorial Note

I pulled this week’s note around the developments that feel most useful for understanding where robotics is getting more commercial, more operational, and easier to evaluate in real terms.

Lead Signal

Travis Kalanick launches a new company called Atoms focused on robotics

Travis Kalanick, the former Uber CEO, has launched a new robotics company named Atoms. The venture's specific focus and initial product details remain undisclosed.

It helps explain where robotics adoption is getting more real, not just more visible.

What Else Mattered

  • Travis Kalanick launches a new company called Atoms focused on robotics

    Travis Kalanick, the former Uber CEO, has launched a new robotics company named Atoms. The venture's specific focus and initial product details remain undisclosed.

    It helps explain where robotics adoption is getting more real, not just more visible.

  • Don’t miss Neuralink pioneer Noland Arbaugh keynote at the 2026 Robotics Summit

    Noland Arbaugh, a pioneering Neuralink patient, is confirmed as a keynote speaker for the 2026 Robotics Summit. His firsthand experience with a brain-computer interface offers a unique perspective for the robotics community.

    It offers a useful read on where deployment, platform strategy, or market momentum may move next.

  • Robot Dogs Are Protecting Data Centers. Operators Are Seeing Payoffs.

    Legged robots are now patrolling data center floors, moving beyond pilot programs into active security and inspection roles. Early operators report measurable returns on investment from these deployments.

    It offers a useful read on where deployment, platform strategy, or market momentum may move next.

  • Humanoid robotics developers must address a wide range of applications

    Argues humanoid robotics developers must look beyond a single killer app. Success hinges on designing versatile platforms capable of addressing a wide range of industrial and commercial applications.

    It offers a useful read on where deployment, platform strategy, or market momentum may move next.

  • Rivian’s RJ Scaringe thinks we’re doing robots all wrong

    Rivian CEO RJ Scaringe critiques the prevailing approach to robotics development. He argues for a fundamental shift in how the industry builds and deploys autonomous systems.

    It offers a useful read on where deployment, platform strategy, or market momentum may move next.

What To Watch

The next thing I’ll be watching is whether these signals turn into repeatable deployments, clearer category leaders, and better evidence that robotics buyers are moving from curiosity to operating habit.

You’re receiving this because you signed up for Robot Weekly at robot.tv.

Read more at news.robot.tv.

Issue archive: https://robot.tv/newsletters/robot-weekly-2026-03-17.html

robot.tv, 8 The Green, Suite 4000, Dover, DE 19901, USA

item.title: Robot Weekly — Mar 11, 2026
item.description: Here is a focused weekly briefing of the most important robotics stories, platform moves, and deployment signals.
item.content:encoded:
Robot Weekly — Mar 11, 2026

Mar 11, 2026

Here is a focused weekly briefing of the most important robotics stories, platform moves, and deployment signals.

Top stories

  • OpenAI robotics head resigns over Pentagon deal

    A senior robotics departure at OpenAI matters because leadership changes around defense-linked decisions can reshape how teams, partners, and observers read the company's priorities in physical AI

  • Plug-and-Play AI: Transforming robotics with modular skills

    Modular robotics skills matter because physical AI becomes more deployable when teams can reuse capabilities across tasks instead of rebuilding each workflow from scratch

  • These robots are coming for the jobs no one wants — and could fill workforce gaps

    Workforce-gap robotics stories matter because automation wins fastest where jobs are repetitive, difficult to staff, or operationally unattractive rather than where robots chase the broadest labor replacement narrative

  • Humanoid developer Agility Robotics rebrands

    An Agility Robotics rebrand matters because company positioning in the humanoid market now affects recruiting, customer expectations, and how seriously operators interpret the path from pilot programs to scaled deployments

  • Humanoid warehouse rollouts are shifting from pilot to operations in 2026

    Warehouse humanoids are entering a stricter phase in 2026: operators are moving beyond pilot headlines and starting to judge programs on throughput, intervention rate, safety, and real workflow fit

  • 6 lessons I learned watching a robotics startup die from the inside

    Robotics startup failure stories matter because execution risk in this industry usually comes from focus, deployment discipline, and operating burn, not from a lack of technical ambition alone

  • Why China’s humanoid robot industry is winning the early market

    China's early lead in humanoid robotics is starting to look structural: dense supply chains, faster hardware iteration, and public pilot visibility are helping local teams move faster than many rivals

  • How to integrate collaborative robots into existing production lines without disruption

    Collaborative robot integration works best when teams redesign flow, safety, and operator handoffs around a real bottleneck instead of dropping a cobot into the line as a stand-alone demo

You can also browse the newsroom feed at news.robot.tv.

Issue archive: https://robot.tv/newsletters/robot-weekly-2026-03-11.html
